stem: The nurse is caring for a 72-year-old client in the ICU who is deteriorating despite initial fluid resuscitation for a suspected infection. Based on the client's electronic health record, drag the findings to complete the bowtie. Identify the most likely condition, two priority actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed (Nurses' Notes)
tabs.content:
SBAR Note (06:15):
S: This is the ICU nurse for the 72-year-old male admitted for urosepsis. He remains hypotensive with a MAP of 53 mmHg despite receiving 2 liters of IV fluids.
B: He has a history of hypertension, T2DM, and a penicillin allergy. A central line was placed at 05:30.
A: He is lethargic with cool, mottled skin. Vitals are BP 80/40, HR 120, RR 28. His CVP is 2 mmHg and lactate is 4.5 mmol/L.
R: I am preparing to administer the STAT ceftriaxone and start the norepinephrine infusion to maintain a MAP >65 mmHg.

tabs.title: Vitals & Telemetry
tabs.content:
TimeBP (MAP)HRRRSpO₂ (Source)Temp °F (°C)CVP (mmHg)
03:00100/60 (73)1102495% (Room Air)102.5 (39.2)N/A
04:0090/50 (63)1152694% (2L NC)102.8 (39.3)N/A
05:0085/45 (58)1182793% (2L NC)103.0 (39.4)N/A
06:0080/40 (53)1202892% (2L NC)103.2 (39.6)2

Telemetry: Sinus Tachycardia

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Admission Assessment (03:15):
General: Appears acutely ill, flushed face.
Neurological: GCS 14 (A&O x3, confused to time). Pupils equal, round, reactive to light.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic with regular rhythm. S1/S2 audible. Pulses 2+ in all extremities. Capillary refill <3 seconds.
Respiratory: Tachypneic but unlabored. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Gastrointestinal: Abdomen soft, non-tender, bowel sounds normoactive.
Skin: Warm, dry, intact.

Focused Assessment (06:00):
Neurological: GCS 12 (lethargic, opens eyes to voice, confused speech).
Cardiovascular: Sinus tachycardia on monitor. Pulses weak and thready (1+ peripherally).
Skin: Cool and clammy to touch. Mottling noted on bilateral lower extremities.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
Portable Chest X-ray (AP View) - 04:30
Indication: Fever, hypotension, rule out pneumonia.
Findings: Lungs are clear without evidence of consolidation, effusion, or significant pulmonary edema. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits for a portable examination. No acute osseous abnormalities are identified.
Impression: No acute cardiopulmonary process.

tabs.title: Care Plan (Orders)
tabs.content:
Order Date/TimeActionFrequency/Status
Today 06:10Norepinephrine infusion, start at 2 mcg/min, titrate to MAP >65 mmHgSTAT/Continuous
Today 06:05Ceftriaxone 1 g in 100 mL NSSTAT, then every 24 hours
Today 03:10Lactated Ringers 1000 mL IV bolusSTAT, may repeat x1
Today 03:05Obtain Blood Cultures x2 sites, Urine CultureSTAT
Today 03:05Insert 2 large-bore peripheral IVsSTAT
Today 03:05Admit to Intensive Care UnitStanding
Today 03:05Vital signs and continuous cardiac monitoringEvery 15 minutes
Today 03:05Strict Intake & OutputHourly

hoverRationales.act_01: Correct. Septic shock causes profound vasodilation. Norepinephrine, a potent vasopressor, is required to increase systemic vascular resistance and raise the mean arterial pressure to restore organ perfusion.
hoverRationales.act_02: Correct. Septic shock is a life-threatening response to infection. Prompt administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics is critical to treat the underlying cause, control the infection, and improve survival outcomes.
hoverRationales.act_03: Incorrect. Furosemide is a diuretic that would remove volume from a client who is already severely hypotensive and volume-depleted (CVP 2 mmHg). This action would worsen shock and is contraindicated.
hoverRationales.act_04: Incorrect. This position provides only a transient increase in blood pressure and can compromise respiratory function and increase intracranial pressure. It is not a recommended intervention for managing shock.
hoverRationales.cond_01: Correct. The client has a suspected infection (urosepsis) plus persistent hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) despite fluid resuscitation and a lactate >2 mmol/L. This clinical picture is the definition of septic shock.
hoverRationales.cond_02: Incorrect. Cardiogenic shock is due to pump failure and would present with signs of fluid overload, such as a high CVP and pulmonary edema. This client's low CVP of 2 mmHg rules out this condition.
hoverRationales.cond_03: Incorrect. Anaphylactic shock is an allergic reaction, typically presenting with urticaria, angioedema, or wheezing after exposure to an allergen. This client's presentation is infectious in origin.
hoverRationales.param_01: Correct. Mean arterial pressure is the primary therapeutic target when titrating vasopressors. Maintaining a MAP ≥65 mmHg is essential for ensuring adequate perfusion to vital organs during shock.
hoverRationales.param_02: Correct. Serum lactate is a direct indicator of anaerobic metabolism resulting from tissue hypoperfusion. Monitoring lactate levels is crucial for assessing the client's response to resuscitation efforts.
hoverRationales.param_03: Incorrect. While important for long-term fluid balance assessment, daily weight is not a priority parameter for guiding immediate hemodynamic resuscitation in the acute phase of septic shock.
hoverRationales.param_04: Incorrect. While CVP provides some information on preload, it is a static measurement and a poor predictor of fluid responsiveness. MAP and lactate are more critical for guiding immediate therapy.

clinicalPearls: Septic shock is defined by persistent hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) requiring vasopressors despite adequate fluid resuscitation, along with a serum lactate >2 mmol/L.
The Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines prioritize early administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics (within 1 hour) and vasopressors for refractory hypotension.
Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock due to its potent alpha-adrenergic effects, which increase systemic vascular resistance.
A low Central Venous Pressure (CVP) in a hypotensive patient suggests hypovolemia or distributive shock, whereas a high CVP suggests cardiogenic shock.

questionTrap.trapDescription: The trap is focusing on fluid status alone and choosing interventions like diuretics or positions that manipulate preload. The client's refractory hypotension after fluid boluses indicates the primary problem is now vasodilation, requiring vasopressors.
questionTrap.strategy: Recognize the signs of refractory shock (hypotension despite fluids). Prioritize interventions that address the underlying pathophysiology—vasodilation (needs vasopressors) and infection (needs antibiotics)—over secondary fluid management concerns.

mnemonic.backsideText: S-Start Antibiotics. E-Enteral fluids (IV). P-Pressors (Norepinephrine). T-Target MAP >65. I-Identify source. C-Cultures first.

pedagogy.nclexCategory: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential
